name: TensorFeed AI Gear
description: Curated, human-reviewed catalog of AI-relevant consumer hardware. Companion to https://tensorfeed.ai/gear with affiliate plumbing stripped.

products.name: Lenovo Legion Pro 7i Gen 10 (16-inch, RTX 5090)
products.blurb: A 24GB VRAM RTX 5090 laptop that runs 30B parameter models locally in 4-bit without choking, and trains LoRA adapters on consumer datasets overnight.
products.description: Lenovo's flagship gaming and AI workstation pairs Intel's 24-core Core Ultra 9 275HX with a mobile RTX 5090 (24GB GDDR7, 175W TGP), a combo that punches well above most desktop tower CPUs. The 16-inch 2.5K OLED runs at 240Hz, which matters less for AI than the thermals: Lenovo's Coldfront cooling sustains the GPU at full power for long inference sessions.
specs: Intel Core Ultra 9 275HX, 24 cores
NVIDIA RTX 5090 Laptop GPU, 24GB GDDR7, up to 175W TGP
32GB or 64GB DDR5-5600 (configurable)
1TB or 2TB PCIe Gen4 NVMe SSD
16-inch WQXGA OLED, 240Hz, 500 nits, Wi-Fi 7
products.aiUseCase: Local LLMs up to 30B 4-bit quantized, Claude Code at full speed, Stable Diffusion XL, LoRA fine-tuning on 7B-13B models

products.name: ASUS ROG Strix SCAR 18 (2026) G835
products.blurb: An 18-inch mobile workstation with a 24GB RTX 5090 and up to 128GB of DDR5, which means you can hold a full 70B model in system RAM and stream layers to the GPU for hybrid inference.
products.description: The SCAR 18 leans heavier into the AI workstation pitch than any other Strix model. The standout is the 128GB max RAM ceiling and 8TB max storage, both user upgradeable, alongside the world's first 4K 240Hz Mini LED laptop panel. Vapor chamber cooling with liquid metal on both CPU and GPU lets the RTX 5090 hold its 175W TGP under sustained load, which is the actual gating factor on long training jobs.
specs: Intel Core Ultra 9 290HX Plus, 24 cores
NVIDIA RTX 5090 Laptop GPU, 24GB GDDR7, 175W TGP
Up to 128GB DDR5-6400 (64GB + 64GB SO-DIMM)
Up to 8TB SSD (4TB + 4TB)
18-inch 4K Mini LED, 240Hz, 1600 nits peak, 2000+ dimming zones
products.aiUseCase: Local LLMs up to 30B fully on GPU, 70B with CPU offload, Stable Diffusion 3.5, multi-day fine-tuning runs with adequate thermals

products.name: Razer Blade 18 (2026)
products.blurb: Razer's flagship configures up to 128GB DDR5 alongside the 24GB RTX 5090, putting it in the same hybrid-inference tier as the SCAR 18 but in a sleeker CNC aluminum chassis.
products.description: The 2026 refresh moves the Blade 18 to Intel's Core Ultra 9 290HX Plus and offers GPUs from RTX 5070 Ti up to RTX 5090 (200W thermal budget). The 18-inch dual-mode display flips between UHD+ at 240Hz and FHD+ at 440Hz. You pay a steep premium over the SCAR for the build quality, the Thunderbolt 5 port, and the slimmest 18-inch chassis on the market.
specs: Intel Core Ultra 9 290HX Plus, 24 cores, 5.5 GHz boost
NVIDIA RTX 5090 Laptop GPU, 24GB GDDR7, 200W thermal budget
Up to 128GB DDR5-6400
1TB to 2TB SSD
18-inch dual-mode display (UHD+ 240Hz or FHD+ 440Hz), Thunderbolt 5
products.aiUseCase: Local LLMs 30B on GPU, 70B with CPU offload, Stable Diffusion workflows, on-the-road agent development

products.name: Apple MacBook Pro M5 Max (14-inch and 16-inch)
products.blurb: Up to 128GB of unified memory at 614 GB/s makes the M5 Max the most capable consumer laptop for fitting larger LLMs entirely in memory, and the silent thermals run for hours on battery.
products.description: The M5 Max ships with an 18-core CPU and 32-core GPU, with up to 128GB of unified memory addressable by both CPU and GPU. For agent developers, the win is that a 70B model in 4-bit (around 40GB) fits comfortably alongside Xcode, a browser, and a few Docker containers. MLX and llama.cpp Metal builds are mature; CUDA workflows are not an option.
specs: Apple M5 Max, 18-core CPU (6 efficiency + 12 performance)
32-core GPU with hardware ray tracing and Neural Engine
Up to 128GB unified memory, 614 GB/s bandwidth
Up to 8TB SSD, 14.5 GB/s read/write
14.2-inch or 16.2-inch Liquid Retina XDR, Wi-Fi 7, Thunderbolt 5
products.aiUseCase: Local LLMs up to 70B 4-bit via MLX or llama.cpp, on-device whisper transcription, MLX fine-tuning of 7B-13B models, Claude Code on battery for hours

products.name: Framework Laptop 16 with RTX 5070 Graphics Module
products.blurb: The only modular laptop where the GPU is a user-swappable cartridge, so when the RTX 6070 module ships you upgrade the laptop, not replace it.
products.description: Framework's 16-inch chassis pairs an AMD Ryzen AI 300 series APU with a removable NVIDIA RTX 5070 (8GB or 12GB GDDR7) graphics module that drops in via a Framework expansion bay. The 8GB module is the practical baseline for AI work; the 12GB module is the better fit for 13B class models. Repairability and upgrade path are the actual differentiators, not raw performance.
specs: AMD Ryzen AI 7 350 or Ryzen AI 9 HX 370
NVIDIA RTX 5070 Laptop GPU module, 8GB or 12GB GDDR7, 100W TGP on AC
16GB to 64GB DDR5-5600 (user upgradeable)
512GB to 2TB NVMe SSD, modular expansion cards
16-inch 2.5K 165Hz IPS, swappable input deck
products.aiUseCase: Local LLMs 7B-13B 4-bit, Stable Diffusion 1.5 and SDXL, light fine-tuning with LoRA, agent prototyping with a repairability-first hardware story

products.name: NVIDIA GeForce RTX 5090
products.blurb: 32GB of GDDR7 at 1,792 GB/s makes the 5090 the highest VRAM consumer GPU shipping, fitting a full 30B model in fp8 or a quantized 70B with room for context.
products.description: The Blackwell flagship pushes 21,760 CUDA cores and 170 SMs against a 575W TDP, delivering roughly 27 to 35 percent faster gaming than the 4090 and a similar lift on AI inference. The 32GB VRAM ceiling is the load-bearing spec for local LLM work: it crosses the 30B-at-fp8 threshold that the 4090 could not. Availability remains tight in May 2026 and street prices are well above the $1,999 MSRP.
specs: 21,760 CUDA cores, 170 SMs, Blackwell architecture
32GB GDDR7, 512-bit bus, 1,792 GB/s bandwidth
5th gen Tensor cores, 4th gen RT cores, DLSS 4
575W TDP, recommended 1000W PSU
PCIe 5.0 x16, three DisplayPort 2.1, one HDMI 2.1b
products.aiUseCase: Local LLMs up to 70B 4-bit quantized, 30B fp8, Stable Diffusion 3.5 and Flux at full resolution, single-GPU fine-tuning of 13B models

products.name: NVIDIA GeForce RTX 5080
products.blurb: 16GB of GDDR7 at 960 GB/s puts the 5080 right at the 13B-fp16 and 30B-4bit sweet spot for builders who want CUDA without the 5090 tax.
products.description: The 5080 ships 10,752 CUDA cores against a 360W TDP and pairs them with 16GB of GDDR7 on a 256-bit bus. For AI workloads, the practical ceiling is 13B class models in fp16 or 30B models in 4-bit quantization. It is the better value pick if VRAM is the bottleneck and you do not need to touch 70B class work locally.
specs: 10,752 CUDA cores, Blackwell architecture
16GB GDDR7, 256-bit bus, 960 GB/s bandwidth
5th gen Tensor cores, 4th gen RT cores
360W TDP, recommended 850W PSU
PCIe 5.0 x16, three DisplayPort 2.1, one HDMI 2.1b
products.aiUseCase: Local LLMs 13B fp16 or 30B 4-bit quantized, Stable Diffusion XL and 3.5, LoRA fine-tuning on 7B models, Claude Code at desktop speeds

products.name: NVIDIA RTX 6000 Ada Generation
products.blurb: 48GB of ECC GDDR6 in a 300W blower card is the right answer for builders who need a quiet workstation that can hold a 70B 4-bit model entirely on one GPU.
products.description: The RTX 6000 Ada is built on the previous Ada Lovelace architecture (not Blackwell), but the 48GB ECC VRAM and 300W blower form factor are exactly what dual-GPU and rack-mount workstations need. Two of these get you to 96GB of GPU memory in a desktop chassis without exotic cooling. The cost premium over a 5090 is justified mainly by the VRAM ceiling and ECC.
specs: 18,176 CUDA cores, 142 RT cores, 568 Tensor cores
48GB GDDR6 with ECC, 960 GB/s bandwidth
300W TDP, dual-slot blower active cooling
PCIe 4.0 x16, four DisplayPort 1.4a, AV1 encode and decode
Workstation drivers, multi-GPU friendly
products.aiUseCase: Local LLMs up to 70B 4-bit on a single card, 13B fp16, multi-GPU fine-tuning at 96GB and beyond, professional rendering and simulation alongside AI

products.name: Meta Ray-Ban Display
products.blurb: The first mass-market AI glasses with an actual in-lens display, paired with an EMG wristband that reads finger twitches as input, making it the closest thing to a wearable agent UI in 2026.
products.description: Meta's Display generation adds a 600 by 600 monocular in-lens screen and the Meta Neural Band, an EMG wristband that picks up muscle signals from subtle finger movements and translates them into commands. The display sits at 42 pixels per degree across a 20-degree FOV with a 5,000 nit ceiling, which is bright enough to read in direct sun. Sold as a $799 bundle including the Neural Band.
specs: 600 x 600 monocular in-lens display, 90Hz refresh, 20-degree FOV
30 to 5,000 nits brightness, less than 2 percent light leakage
Qualcomm Snapdragon AR1 Gen 1, 12MP camera, 5 mics, dual off-ear speakers
Up to 6 hours mixed use, 30 hours with the case
Meta Neural Band EMG wristband included
products.aiUseCase: Conversational Meta AI agent on the lens, real-time translation, message and notification glanceables, first-person camera for vision-LLM workflows

products.name: Xreal One Pro
products.blurb: A 171-inch virtual 1080p display strapped to your face at 3.1 ounces, ideal for running a Claude Code session on a Steam Deck or laptop while traveling.
products.description: The One Pro uses Sony 0.55-inch Micro OLED panels behind X-Prism optics to project a 171-inch virtual display at 120Hz with a 57-degree field of view. The onboard X1 chip handles 3DoF spatial tracking so the screen can either anchor in space or follow your head. USB-C in, no phone required, no battery in the glasses.
specs: Sony 0.55-inch Micro OLED, 1080p per eye, 120Hz
57-degree FOV, 171-inch virtual display
X1 chip for 3DoF spatial tracking, Real 3D 2D-to-3D conversion
Bose-tuned open-ear speakers, USB-C wired connection
3.1 ounces, IPD 57 to 66mm
products.aiUseCase: Portable second screen for Claude Code, agent development, or LLM chat workflows when paired with a Mac, Steam Deck, ROG Ally, or compatible phone

products.name: Even Realities G1
products.blurb: The lightest in-lens display glasses that pass for normal eyewear, designed around teleprompt, translate, navigate, and AI assistant flows rather than gaming or media.
products.description: Even Realities builds the G1 around a magnesium and titanium frame with tiny optical projectors hidden in the lens edge. There is no camera on the standard model, which makes them less polarizing in public than the Ray-Ban Display, and the daily wear story is genuinely closer to regular glasses. The trade-off is a more limited display and a heavier dependence on the phone app.
specs: Dual in-lens micro-projectors, monochrome green text overlay
Panto or rectangular frame, magnesium fused with sandstone
Hypoallergenic titanium and silicone temple tips
iOS and Android compatible, ambient brightness sensors
Optional Pro translation subscription
products.aiUseCase: Heads-up AI assistant prompts, live translation overlays, teleprompt for content creators, navigation, low-key wearable agent UI for daily wear

products.name: Brilliant Labs Frame
products.blurb: Open source AI glasses with full design files on GitHub, a microOLED, a camera, and Noa, a hackable LLM assistant that turns the frame into a dev kit rather than a finished product.
products.description: Brilliant Labs ships the Frame as a near-40g pair of glasses with a microOLED bonded to a prism in the right lens (640 by 400, 500 nits, 20-degree diagonal FOV), a camera over the nose bridge, and a microphone. The Nordic nRF52840 plus a Lattice CrosslinkNX FPGA handle on-device compute. The pitch is that everything is open source, so you can build your own agent on top of the included Noa assistant.
specs: 640 x 400 microOLED, 500 nits, 20-degree diagonal FOV
Nordic nRF52840 ARM Cortex-M4F, Lattice CrosslinkNX FPGA
Camera on the brim, microphone, IPD 58 to 72mm
Under 40g, all-day battery with Mister Power charger
Fully open source hardware and software (design files on GitHub)
products.aiUseCase: Hackable wearable LLM dev kit, custom on-device vision models, Noa AI assistant out of the box, ideal for agent builders who want to own the stack
